entities.description: A publicly viewable, item-specific uncorrected OCR encoding of Napier's title page, Middle English poem, and reading notes. The header says no OCR correction or content editing was performed, identifies TEI in Libraries Level 1, and links the text to page images.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item ACB1675.0001.001
notes: The inherited bibliographic AUTHOR field names Napier, but he is editor of an anonymous medieval work.
The encoding contains 538 L, 20 manuscript-folio MILESTONE, 15 NOTE1, 78 PB, and no LG elements.
Most of Napier's introduction (pp. vii–xxxii), notes (pp. 19–23), and glossary (pp. 24–41) are represented only by PB links inside divisions typed as omitted front or back matter.

bibliographic.citation: Iacob and Iosep: A Middle English Poem of the Thirteenth Century, ed. Arthur S. Napier (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1916).
bibliographic.title: Iacob and Iosep: A Middle English Poem of the Thirteenth Century

entities.label: Napier 1916 edition of Iacob and Iosep
notes: Napier states that he copied the poem years before Heuser's 1905 edition appeared, then resumed his independent edition because the earlier publication was difficult to access.

The volume contains an English introduction (pp. vii–xxxii), the Middle English text with footnote readings and folio locators (pp. 1–18), notes (pp. 19–23), glossary (pp. 24–41), and two facsimile plates.

Napier supplies the bracketed opening heading, marks direct speech with modern punctuation, prints the manuscript explicit, discusses but rejects uncertain emendations proposed by Heuser, and records the leaf cut out between fols. 6 and 7.

rights.basis: The current U-M item calls the materials public domain in the United States, and Internet Archive marks its 1916 scan with the Public Domain Mark.
rights.component: 1916 printed edition text and apparatus
notes: This record does not claim worldwide status for every modern reproduction or interface asset.

entities.description: Two monochrome plates bound into the 1916 edition, reproducing the consecutive manuscript pages carrying approximately lines 406–459.
entities.label: Napier's double facsimile of Bodley 652 fols. 8v and 9r

rights.basis: The plates were published in the 1916 volume. U-M currently applies No Copyright–United States to the image-bearing item, Internet Archive applies the Public Domain Mark to its scan, and Copyright Office duration guidance describes the maximum term for pre-1978 published copyright as 95 years.
rights.component: two manuscript facsimile plates first published in Napier's 1916 volume, excluding later scan files and interfaces
notes: The historical plates' U.S. status is separate from Digital Bodleian's modern fol. 1r photograph and from provider terms governing later scans.

bibliographic.citation: Wilhelm Heuser, ‘Das frühmittelenglische Josephlied (Ms. Bodl. 652),’ Bonner Beiträge zur Anglistik 17 (1905): 83–121.
bibliographic.title: Das frühmittelenglische Josephlied (Ms. Bodl. 652)

entities.label: Heuser 1905 edition of Das frühmittelenglische Josephlied
notes: This is a distinct prior edition. Napier consulted it but states that his own transcription predated it, so Heuser is not modeled as the text from which Napier copied.

rights.basis: The article and edition were first published in 1905. U.S. Copyright Office duration guidance gives eligible pre-1978 published copyrights a maximum term of 95 years, which had expired before this review.
rights.component: Heuser's 1905 article, edited text, and original scholarly apparatus, excluding later scan files and interfaces
notes: This U.S. content-status record does not determine copyright or contract terms for Internet Archive's later scan derivative, which remains separately unknown.

access.format: mediated reference scans or commissioned imaging

dateStatements.label: Napier's dating of the poem hand
dateStatements.notes: Napier distinguishes this first part's hand from the three-part codex as a whole; the difference from the current institutional end-of-century label remains unresolved.
dateStatements.value: soon after the middle of the thirteenth century

entities.description: The secure manuscript witness of Iacob and Iosep in the first textual component of a composite parchment codex. The poem occupies fols. 1r–10v, and one leaf has been cut out between fols. 6 and 7.

holding.notes: The repository-owned catalog record is used here; scholarly handlists such as ARLIMA and PCMEP remain separate evidence.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 652, fols. 1r–10v

physicalDescription.extent: Iacob and Iosep at fols. 1r–10v; one leaf missing between fols. 6 and 7
notes: Digital Bodleian identifies the object as a composite manuscript from Southwest England and describes the selected fol. 1r as a text page with a decorated initial and red penwork flourishing.
Napier describes three separately written codicological parts, all from second-half thirteenth-century England, and assigns the poem to the first part.
Adjacent codex contents include French Lucidarium, Letter of Prester John, and Chasteau d'Amour texts plus Latin motets; they are not parts of Iacob and Iosep.

rights.basis: The anonymous medieval text was created centuries before 1978 and was published in Heuser's 1905 and Napier's 1916 editions. Current U-M and Internet Archive provider statements treat the published material as free of U.S. copyright restrictions; Copyright Office guidance supplies the relevant finite duration framework for pre-1978 publications.
rights.component: underlying thirteenth-century Iacob and Iosep text at fols. 1r–10v, excluding the physical object and all photographs
notes: Ownership and handling rules for the physical manuscript, and copyright or contract terms for modern photographs, remain separate layers.

access.entityLabel: Beinecke Takamiya MS 32, fol. 3r (candidate Iacob and Iosep fragment)
access.provider: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

dateStatements.label: production of the codex
dateStatements.notes: This dates the codex as cataloged, not an independently dated Jacob-and-Joseph fragment.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century composite literary manuscript whose faded or reused fol. 3r has been proposed as preserving a related Jacob-and-Joseph fragment. Yale's catalog does not identify that folio as Iacob and Iosep, and the exact textual relationship remains tentative.

holding.notes: Scholarship commonly shortens the call number to Takamiya MS 32; the reviewed Yale item manifest gives Takamiya MS 32 1.

entities.label: Beinecke Takamiya MS 32, fol. 3r (candidate Iacob and Iosep fragment)

notes: Yale catalogs the codex as English, circa 1425–1450, in one hand, generally two columns of 39–44 lines, and bound in nineteenth-century red morocco.
Its cataloged contents include the Canterbury Tales, Gower extracts, and several Middle English poems, including a distinct Vision of Tundale; the catalog does not list Iacob and Iosep.
Visual inspection of the public fol. 3r canvas confirms a difficult faded or reused text page but cannot by itself establish its relation to Bodley 652.

rights.basis: Beinecke states that public-domain collection material does not require library permission and that it generally does not hold copyright, but the exact item lacks a specific rights field; image and contractual layers remain separate.
rights.component: underlying medieval manuscript text, excluding modern photography and interface assets

workPortion.label: Candidate related Iacob and Iosep fragment
notes: The proposed relationship to Iacob and Iosep is tentative; this mapping locates the candidate fragment without treating it as a confirmed witness.
The PDF's generated title sheet creates a one-page offset from the 366-canvas IIIF sequence: canvas 11 is PDF page 12.

entities.description: A complete 366-canvas IIIF facsimile with a public PDF rendering; fol. 3r is publicly viewable within it.
entities.label: Yale complete digital facsimile of Takamiya MS 32

next_steps: Obtain Hume's 2021 conference paper or subsequent publication and a specialist diplomatic transcription.
Request enhanced or multispectral imaging if ordinary color images remain illegible, then collate the folio against Bodley 652 and both early editions.
Keep the candidate relation tentative and outside the secure witness count until the textual identity is demonstrated.
openQuestions.question: Does Takamiya MS 32 fol. 3r preserve Iacob and Iosep, a reworked descendant, a cognate Jacob-and-Joseph text, or unrelated material?

next_steps: Consult the full current Bodleian catalog description, LAEME and LALME profiles, and recent palaeographic work on the first codicological part.
Keep witness production, scribal dialect, and work composition locality as separately attributed claims.
openQuestions.question: How should Napier's ‘soon after the middle’ dating and South-West dialect argument for the poem portion be reconciled with Digital Bodleian's end-thirteenth-century and Southwest England labels and more cautious modern original-dialect assessments?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections imaging services for reference or high-resolution scans of fols. 1r–10v.
Record the supplied files and permission terms as a new reproduction entity rather than extending the one-canvas CC BY-NC record.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete, current institutional image set of Bodley 652 fols. 1r–10v be obtained, and under what item-specific terms?

next_steps: Request item-level provenance and rights clarification from U-M Library.
Treat the current NoC-US label as jurisdiction-specific and preserve the legacy notice until the provider resolves the scope.
openQuestions.question: What exact source copy and scanning provenance underlie U-M's 78 page images, and how should the legacy generic restriction notice be reconciled with the current NoC-US item statement for redistribution outside the United States?

next_steps: Search union catalogs by title/editor, inspect a physical copy, and compare its title and copyright pages, type setting, pagination, preliminaries, plates, and colophon against the 1916 issue.
Retain it as a separate qualified version and do not make it U-M's source without copy-specific evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which publisher and physical issue does ARLIMA's reported 1998 reprint of Napier's edition refer to, and did it contain any reset, revision, or new matter?

relations.subjectLabel: Yale complete digital facsimile of Takamiya MS 32

reviewNotes: The cataloged work is the anonymous Middle English poem, not Bodley 652 as a composite codex, Napier's edition, U-M's OCR, or any digital facsimile.

MS. Bodl. 652 is the current shelfmark; Summary Catalogue 2306 is retained as a historical catalog designation. Yale's current item record gives Takamiya MS 32 1, while scholarship and the collection guide use Takamiya MS 32.
The one-canvas Digital Bodleian object is a selected image of fol. 1r, not a complete facsimile. Napier's two plates likewise reproduce only fols. 8v and 9r.
ARLIMA reports a 1998 reprint of Napier, which is modeled separately because no evidence shows that U-M encoded or scanned that reprint.
Current U-M No Copyright–United States language and the legacy XML's generic copyright/restriction notice are both preserved at their own layers and dates.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy or version of University of Michigan CME item ACB1675.0001.001, an uncorrected OCR/TEI-in-Libraries Level 1 encoding of Arthur S. Napier's 1916 Clarendon Press edition. Napier independently transcribed the secure witness, Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 652, fols. 1r–10v, while consulting Wilhelm Heuser's distinct 1905 edition; Napier's volume also contains two selected manuscript facsimiles. U-M links 78 page images of the whole printed volume but OCR-encodes chiefly the title page and 538-line poem, omitting the introduction, notes, and glossary as text. Separate public reproductions include an Internet Archive scan of Napier, a one-canvas Digital Bodleian image of fol. 1r, and a complete Yale facsimile of Takamiya MS 32. The faded text at Takamiya fol. 3r is only a candidate related Jacob-and-Joseph fragment, not a confirmed second witness.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Probably second half of the thirteenth century; one corpus proposes 1260–1290
catalogSummary.region: England; original dialect and place of composition unresolved
catalogSummary.title: Iacob and Iosep

catalogingSubject.label: Iacob and Iosep
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English verse retelling of the Jacob and Joseph narrative, extant as 538 lines plus a Latin explicit in Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 652, fols. 1r–10v. This abstract work is distinct from that composite manuscript, Arthur S. Napier's 1916 edition and its later reprint, the University of Michigan OCR and page images, the repository XML artifact, and a possibly related but unresolved fragment on fol. 3r of Beinecke Takamiya MS 32.

contentParts.label: Iacob and Iosep
notes: The missing leaf is a physical lacuna, not a separately modeled content part. Napier's introduction, notes, glossary, and facsimile plates are edition apparatus outside the medieval work boundary.
contentParts.summary: The continuous verse narrative from its address to listeners through Jacob's reunion with Joseph, followed by the Latin explicit.

dateStatements.display: Probably second half of the thirteenth century
notes: The date is a scholarly estimate for composition, not the 1916 edition date or the date of any digital object.
Retained as an attributed project estimate rather than collapsed into an exact date.

extent.description: One continuous extant poem of 538 L elements in the repository encoding and 538 printed lines in Napier's edition. Twenty manuscript-folio milestones mark fols. 1r–10v; one missing leaf between fols. 6 and 7 means this is extant rather than planned or original extent. The XML has no LG elements, so no stanza or verse-group count is asserted.

formStatements.description: A 538-line narrative in rhyming couplets, mixing predominantly alexandrines with septenaries and some four-stress lines; some alliteration is present.

genres.label: Romance-inflected biblical poetry
notes: This records formal and cultural participation in romance, not a claim that the poem is simply a secular romance.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The surrounding codex contains French and Latin works, Napier's apparatus is modern English, and the closing formula is Latin; none makes the cataloged poem as a whole multilingual.
Napier is editor and transcriber of the immediate print source; the inherited XML AUTHOR label must not make him author of the medieval poem.
The 538-line extent is extant and encoding-specific. One manuscript leaf is missing, and PCMEP's displayed 583 count is not adopted without a reproducible explanation.
Bodley 652 is a composite codex with adjacent French and Latin works. Those codex languages, dates, and subjects are not assigned to Iacob and Iosep as a whole.
The possible Takamiya fragment remains outside the secure witness count pending textual verification.

next_steps: Consult Cathy Hume's 2021 paper and any subsequent publication or catalog revision.
Obtain a specialist transcription and multispectral or enhanced images of fol. 3r, then collate it against Bodley 652 and Napier's text.
openQuestions.question: Is the difficult text on fol. 3r of Beinecke Takamiya MS 32 a second, reworked, or otherwise related witness of Iacob and Iosep, and what exact passage and transmission relationship does it preserve?

next_steps: Review LAEME/LALME profiles and current scholarship on the Bodley 652 scribe and rhyme language.
Retain broad England cultural origin until composition-locality evidence is independent of the surviving witness.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's original dialect and place of composition be distinguished from the language and production locality of Bodley 652?

next_steps: Compare Murdoch's and Hume's arguments with the manuscript's codicological context and contemporary performance evidence.
Do not convert interpretive social profiles into known creator identities or certain audience metadata.
openQuestions.question: What can be established about the anonymous poet and intended audience beyond competing minstrel, clerically trained poet, household chaplain, baronial, and knightly hypotheses?

next_steps: Compare the lacuna and distinctive episodes with the relevant Genesis, French Bible, Cursor Mundi, and iconographic traditions.
Keep possible source relationships qualified rather than forcing a single direct exemplar.
openQuestions.question: What narrative material occupied the lost leaf between Bodley 652 fols. 6v and 7r, and which biblical, French, iconographic, or vernacular sources best explain the poem's non-Vulgate details?

notes: The broad English cultural origin does not prove a specific place of composition. Southwestern England and Gloucestershire describe scholarly dialect or witness-localization claims that remain uncertain for the abstract work.
Modern proposals that the poet was a minstrel, a clerically trained poet, or a household chaplain are interpretive and remain unresolved.

scope.description: Vernacular verse reshaping of the Jacob and Joseph narrative in Genesis 37–47 and related medieval biblical traditions
Transcription of Bodley 652, fols. 1r–10v, with readings and emendations in footnotes

subjects.label: Providence, fate, and free will
notes: The poem repeatedly attributes outcomes to God's will, though modern accounts differ on how overtly didactic the work is.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English poem retelling the Joseph cycle with Jacob as a persistent emotional center. Joseph's brothers sell him into Egypt; he rises at Pharaoh's court through dream interpretation, tests the brothers during famine, reveals himself, and reunites the family. The poem reshapes Genesis with vernacular, courtly, household, and romance-inflected details rather than translating the Vulgate mechanically.

Bodley 652 preserves the secure text on fols. 1r–10v, but one leaf has been cut out between fols. 6 and 7, so the extant 538-line poem is incomplete. A difficult, erased or reused text on fol. 3r of the fifteenth-century Delamere Chaucer (Takamiya MS 32) has been proposed as a related Jacob and Joseph fragment, but its exact textual relationship remains unresolved and it is not counted as a secure second witness here.

notes: The wording is preserved in the manuscript explicit; Napier supplied the same wording as a bracketed heading because the manuscript has no opening title.
This is an edition-layer title; its thirteenth-century dating is not an original medieval subtitle.
titles.value: Iacob and Iosep: A Middle English Poem of the Thirteenth Century
Wolle ȝe nou ihere wordes swiþe gode / Of one patriarke after Noees flode?

notes: The formula is too brief to make the whole work multilingual; discovery language remains Middle English.

scope.description: Brief Latin closing formula following the Middle English poem

Joseph's brothers sell him into Egypt; he rises at Pharaoh's court through dream interpretation, tests the brothers during famine, reveals himself, and reunites the family. The poem reshapes Genesis with vernacular, courtly, household, and romance-inflected details rather than translating the Vulgate mechanically.
